coredata.dc:title: “Turn ON-OFF-ON” photoluminescence detection of EDTA using Moringa oleifera gum-derived carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Anisha
Karmakar, Ankita
Varanasi, Swambabu
coredata.dc:description: This study offers a simplistic approach to hydrothermally synthesizing photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) using Moringa oleifera Gum (MOG) to detect and quantify EDTA. The as-synthesized Moringa oleifera Gum-derived CDs (MOG-CDs) exhibited an average size of 3.41±0.44 nm and are amorphous. They are naturally doped with calcium and sulphur. Functional groups linked with these heteroatoms and oxygenated linkers are responsible for excellent water dispersibility, good stability, and remarkable optical properties of MOG-CDs. The MOG-CDs demonstrated a high quantum yield of 27.53%. The PL intensity of MOG-CDs was selectively quenched by the introduction of Pb2+ ions, which were further used as a passivating agent for EDTA detection, i.e., the quenched PL can be restored by the addition of EDTA into MOG-CD+Pb2+ system. This Turn “ON-OFF-ON” strategy was used to quantify EDTA with a LOD of 2.6 ppb (8.9 nM). The practicality and viability of the proposed photoluminescent probe were validated by quantifying EDTA in actual samples. The results demonstrated favourable spiked recoveries ranging from 92.59% to 108.69%, indicating a high level of accuracy. Additionally, it displayed excellent reproducibility, as suggested by relative standard deviation (RSD) <5.51%. In parallel, an "IMPLICATION" Boolean logic gate was constructed by employing the PL restoration of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ sensing probe in the presence of EDTA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Moringa oleifera gum
Carbon dots
Photoluminescent sensor
Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid
Logic gates

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of EDTA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image, b) Particle size distribution, c) XPS spectrum [21], d) FTIR spectrum, e) XRD spectrum [21], and f) Raman spectrum of MOG-CDs [21].
a) UV–vis spectrum, and b) Excitation-dependent PL spectrum of MOG-CDs.
a) PL spectra, and b) UV–vis spectra of selectivity study of MOG-CDs, and c) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of MOG-CD and MOG-CD+Pb2+ system [21].

a) PL spectra, and b) UV–vis spectra of selectivity study of MOG-CD+Pb2+ systems, and c) Bar plot displaying selectivity of EDTA (1 mM) over interferants (5 mM) (red bar), and influence of interferants in MOG-CD+Pb2++EDTA system (pink bar).
a) PL spectra of PL intensity vs concentration of EDTA, and b) I/Io vs concentration of EDTA graph.
a) PL spectra of MOG-CD solution in the presence of Pb2+ ions and EDTA, b) Two-input “IMPLICATION” logic gates and truth tables based on PL spectroscopy results.

head.ce:title: “Turn ON-OFF-ON” photoluminescence detection of EDTA using Moringa oleifera gum-derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, West Bengal, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, West Bengal, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study offers a simplistic approach to hydrothermally synthesizing photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) using Moringa oleifera Gum (MOG) to detect and quantify EDTA. The as-synthesized Moringa oleifera Gum-derived CDs (MOG-CDs) exhibited an average size of 3.41±0.44 nm and are amorphous. They are naturally doped with calcium and sulphur. Functional groups linked with these heteroatoms and oxygenated linkers are responsible for excellent water dispersibility, good stability, and remarkable optical properties of MOG-CDs. The MOG-CDs demonstrated a high quantum yield of 27.53%. The PL intensity of MOG-CDs was selectively quenched by the introduction of Pb2+ ions, which were further used as a passivating agent for EDTA detection, i.e., the quenched PL can be restored by the addition of EDTA into MOG-CD+Pb2+ system. This Turn “ON-OFF-ON” strategy was used to quantify EDTA with a LOD of 2.6 ppb (8.9 nM). The practicality and viability of the proposed photoluminescent probe were validated by quantifying EDTA in actual samples. The results demonstrated favourable spiked recoveries ranging from 92.59% to 108.69%, indicating a high level of accuracy. Additionally, it displayed excellent reproducibility, as suggested by relative standard deviation (RSD) <5.51%. In parallel, an "IMPLICATION" Boolean logic gate was constructed by employing the PL restoration of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ sensing probe in the presence of EDTA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and its salts (EDTA) are conventional heavy metal chelating agents. Owing to their exceptional ability to form soluble and stable chelates or complexes with metal ions, they are extensively applied as stabilizing agents and antioxidants in many domains. Some of these include textiles, food, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and so on [1]. EDTA is an incessant pollutant to the environment owing to its poor biodegradability. EDTA is reported to cause allergic contact dermatitis on human skin and blood abnormalities. Overuse of EDTA may result in a build-up of free radicals in the body, making it more susceptible to obesity and putting more strain on the kidneys and other organs [2–4]. Thus, it is imperative to investigate the existence of even minute amounts of EDTA. Conventional techniques for EDTA detection involve voltammetry, gas chromatography, ion chromatography, AAS, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), titrimetry, and many more. However, these methods suffer from limitations in their ability to detect EDTA at low concentrations, particularly when approaching the micromolar (µM) level. Thus, EDTA detection at lower limits requires new, more sensitive methods, and PL-based sensing mechanisms can pave the way for a resolution to this setback [5].

Carbon dots (CDs) have been acknowledged as an exceptional photoluminescent probe since its discovery by Xu et al. in 2004. This intriguing new 0-D member of the family of carbon nanomaterials has been used in various applications because of their outstanding photostability, biocompatibility, photochemical stability, water dispersibility, and low toxicity [6,7]. CD is a quasi-spherical material with a characteristic size below 10 nm [8]. They are comparable to organic dye molecules, metal nanoparticles, and semiconductor quantum dots because of their distinctive features [9]. So far, chemical reagents have dominated CD synthesis, and reports of using biomass resources are rare. Green synthesis of CDs using a new natural precursor will significantly contribute to this field.

In the literature, many reports have investigated plant gums as the source of CDs. Some of these are gum tragacanth [10–12], cashew gum[13], neem gum [14], peach gum [15], and guar gum [16]. Moringa oleifera leaves [17] and roots [18] have been used for the synthesis of CDs. Yet, Moringa oleifera gum (MOG) has never been exploited for synthesising CDs despite being used as a source of activated carbon [19,20]. We are the pioneers in using MOG for synthesizing CDs and employing it for lead detection [21]. MOG has plenty of functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, sulfoxide, and calcium-containing groups [22]. These oxygenated groups are responsible for the hydrophilic nature of CDs and even enhance their fluorescence. Sulphur-containing functional groups are also accountable for excellent fluorescence. These linkers indicate that other than the applications mentioned above, MOG has the potential to be used for sensor applications [19].

Although EDTA has been crosslinked with CDs to enhance their fluorescent properties, limited reports are available on the detection of EDTA using heavy metal-passivated CD-based fluorescent sensors. In the first-ever study by Yang et al. in 2018, zinc (Zn)-passivated carbon dots were used to detect Zn2+ and EDTA [20]. Another report by Wen et al. in 2021 used a “turn off-on” strategy for the detection of Cobalt (Co2+) and EDTA [22]. Xu et al. passivated the CD solution with copper (Cu2+) ion and used it for the detection of EDTA [23,24]. These are a few investigations that have been reported to detect EDTA using heavy metal-passivated CDs. This study aims to develop a PL sensing method using Pb2+-passivated CDs to detect and quantify EDTA, which was never reported, with LOD values fewer than reported in the literature, suited to more practical applications.

This study has two broad aims. Firstly, CDs were synthesised hydrothermally using MOG and passivated with Pb2+ ions. Secondly, lead passiavted MOG-CDs are then used to detect and quantify EDTA. The obtained MOG-derived CDs (MOG-CDs) were meticulously characterized by various techniques to know their surface morphology, size, functionalities, and optical properties. The stability study of MOG-CDs was investigated regarding ionic strength, pH, and storage time. Initially, the PL of the MOG-CD solution is quenched by adding Pb2+ ions into it, and then the determination of EDTA is realized based on PL recovery. This is the first-ever report that has involved Pb2+-passivated MOG-CDs for the Turn ON-OFF-ON detection of EDTA.
The Supplementary file provides the required materials and characterization techniques.

A homogenous MOG solution was prepared by grinding and dissolving 1 g of dried MOG in 100 mL of DIW by stirring at 500 rpm at 60°C for 12 hrs. The solution was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and placed in an oven at 200°C for 8 hours (this optimal reaction temperature is applied after analysing its effect on the particle size). After the autoclave cooled down, the solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the larger particles. The obtained golden supernatant was purified via dialysis with the membrane of 10 kDa molecular weight cut-off. The purified MOG-CD solution was then stored at 4°C for future analysis. Fig. 1 illustrates a simple hydrothermal process for the synthesis of MOG-CDs.

The stability of the MOG-CD solution was investigated by analyzing the PL response concerning NaCl concentrations, pH, and storage time. The effect of varying NaCl concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 mM on the PL intensity of the MOG-CD solution was studied to determine the effect of the ionic strength. To determine the effect of pH on the PL intensity, the pH of the MOG-CD solution was adjusted between 1 and 13 using 0.1 N H2SO4 and 0.1 N NaOH as necessary. The PL Intensity of the MOG-CD solution stored in a refrigerator for 45 days was measured at intervals of 5 days to verify the shelf life of the solution.

This study investigated the PL recovery effect of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system with different EDTA concentrations. Initially, 10 ppm of Lead (II) acetate trihydrate solution (0.5 mL) was added into separate aliquots of MOG-CD solution (3 mL). A 0.05 M stock solution of EDTA disodium salt dihydrate was prepared, and the solution pH was adjusted to 8 using 0.1 M NaOH solution. At pH 8, EDTA exists primarily in the form of HY3-. Then, different concentrations (0–100 ppm) of EDTA solutions were separately added to the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system, and the PL response was measured. For all samples, excitation occurred at 405 nm.

A selectivity study was conducted by replacing the EDTA solution (1 mM) with other analytes (ascorbic acid, dopamine, fructose, sucrose, glucose, urea, and uric acid). This study was also conducted using UV–vis spectroscopy. The MOG-CD solution used for this study was 75-fold diluted. Along with this, MOG-CD+Pb2++EDTA systems’ anti-interference capability was also assessed by recording their PL response with and without the presence of interferents.

The selectivity study of Pb2+ and EDTA was also carried out using UV–vis spectroscopy. To study the Pb2+ selectivity, 0.5 mL of each ion solution (1 mM) was added in separate vials to the MOG-CD solution (3 mL). The MOG-CD solution used was 50-fold diluted.

For real sample analysis of EDTA, two different water sources (tap water and drinking water), cold drink, and facewash were used. Tap and drinking water were collected from our department and used directly without any pre-treatment. Facewash was mixed with DIW and sonicated, and the supernatant was used for the analysis. All samples were spiked with different known concentrations (0.5 ppm and 10 ppm) of EDTA, and the PL spectra were recorded. These results were also validated using High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

The MOG-CD cell cytotoxicity was measured using the MTT assay method. RAW 264.7 cells were seeded in 96-well cell culture plates on the day of the assay to attain 70–80% confluency. The cell culture medium was RPMI-1640 with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The assay used a stock solution of MOG-CD in RPMI-1640 medium serially diluted (without phenol red) to ensure consistent medium component concentrations across all wells. After replacing the culture medium with a triplicate MOG-CD solution, the cells were incubated for 24 hrs at standard conditions. A serum-free RPMI-1640 medium contained 0.5 mg/mL fresh MTT solution. Post-incubation, the medium was removed from each well without disrupting the cell monolayer. The cells were incubated for 3 hrs at 37°C with MTT solution (10% of the culture medium volume) in each well. Viable cells will result in the formation of purple/blue formazan crystals upon exposure to MTT. After discarding the MTT solution, the plates were gently shaken with DMSO to dissolve formazan crystals. After that, a microplate reader recorded formazan solution absorbance at 570–590 nm. Absorbance directly correlates with viable cell count. Cultured DMSO cells were used as a control. Cell viability (%) was calculated using the following formula [23].
Cell viability (%) = (Absorbance of the treated cells / Absorbance of the control cells) × 100%

MOG is composed of polysaccharide units, which are mainly derived from hemicellulose degradation [24]. In literature, the synthesis of CDs based on cellulose/hemicellulose-based precursors has been extensively studied, but the reason for its formation is still indistinct. However, one plausible mechanism, consistent with other reports involving similar precursors, is discussed below.

This synthetic pathway begins with hydrolysis of cellulose/hemicellulose. Under hydrothermal conditions and in the presence of H+ ions, cellulose/hemicellulose gets converted to oligosaccharides and glucose. Subsequently, glucose and other saccharides undergo dehydration reaction, resulting in the formatio of low molecular weight compounds, including furfural intermediates and 5-HMF. These primary dehydration products then polymerize and transform into aromatic polymers, ultimately condensing into carbonaceous material. The resulting carbonized nuclei, soluble in aqueous solvent, turn into CDs [25–27].

The TEM image of MOG-CDs, as displayed in Fig. 2a, indicates that the particle size is less than 10 nm. The average size of the MOG-CDs was determined to be 3.41±0.44 nm based on a statistical analysis of over 50 random particles, and a narrow particle size distribution from 2.4 to 4.4 nm is shown in Fig. 2b.

XPS and FTIR examined the composition and identity of the surface functionalities. The full XPS spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2c, suggested that MOG-CDs are mainly composed of C, Ca, S, and O. Four prominent peaks at 284, 349, 532, and 170 eV are attributed to C1s, Ca2p, O1s, and S2p, respectively, and their high-resolution spectrum is given in Fig. S1. The three peaks at 283.6, 284.9, and 287.3 eV can be identified in the high resolution of the C1s spectrum allocated in Fig. S1a, and they are associated with C-C/CC/C-S-C, C-H/C-OH/C-S, and C-O/CO/CS, respectively [28]. Fig. S1b represents the high-resolution O1s spectrum, which revealed two peaks at 531.5 and 533 eV corresponding to CO/C-O and C-OH/C-O-C/-COOH, respectively [29]. The high-resolution Ca2p spectrum displayed in Fig. S1c exhibits five peaks at 344.85, 347.63, 348.85, 351.61, and 354 eV, indicating the presence of Ca-O, Ca2p3/2, and Ca2p1/2. The three deconvoluted peaks of the S2p spectrum placed at 167, 169.61, and 170.39 eV reveal the association with oxidized sulfur, including sulfonate [30,31]. These elements are inherited from the precursor and are self-doped on the surface of the MOG-CDs, as shown in Fig. S3.

The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2d offered comprehensive information regarding the surface functional groups associated with these elements. The sharp peak observed at around 3419 cm−1 designates the vibrational stretching of the O-H bonds. The absorption band in the vicinity of 2927 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H bonding. The intense peak noted at 1631 cm−1 is indicative of the stretching vibration exhibited by C-O, CO, and CC bonds. The spectral peaks observed within the range of 1247–1518 cm−1 are reflective of the existence of CC aromatic bonds, C-C and C-O-C stretching vibrations, -COO groups, Ca-O bonds, and CH3 bends [32].

Asymmetric stretching of C-O-C, C-O, CO, Ca-OH, CS, SO, and C-H bonds induces the strong peak around 1098 cm-1. The interaction between Ca²⁺ ions and the -OH and -COOH functional groups of the MOG-CDs occurs due to the negative charges on these moieties. Ca²⁺ ions tend to bind to the negatively charged carboxylic groups of CDs through a charge difference [33]. Additionally, the carbonyl oxygen donates a lone pair, forming a coordinate bond with calcium [34]. The band of peaks in the region 473–905 cm−1 is associated with SO4 2-, C-S and Ca-O [35–37]. These functional groups have the potential to improve the optical properties of PW-CDs. The dominance and interaction of these linkers can be modified for sensing and other applications.

Fig. 2e portrays the XRD spectra, which have two broad peaks near 17.4° and 45.34° corresponding to the (002) and (100) lattice spacing of graphite, respectively. The peaks further indicate that the MOG-CDs are amorphous in nature [38]. The two peaks in the Raman spectrum, as displayed in Fig. 2f, correspond to the D and G bands, and they are located at 1383 and 1579 cm−1. These peaks signify the disordered D band of sp3 carbon and the crystalline G band of sp2 carbon, respectively. The G and D band intensities ratio (IG/ID) is 1.07, greater than 1, which suggests that MOG-CD exhibits a good degree of graphitization. Moreover, it confirms the existence of amorphous structures [38].

The UV–vis spectrum of MOG-CDs from Fig. 3a shows a single peak at 279 nm with a tail that reaches the visible range. The peak is due to the π-π* transition of the CC bonds, while the extending tail represents the n- π* transition. The functional groups present on the surface of the MOG-CDs are responsible for this behaviour [39]. Fig. 3b shows the PL emission spectra over the excitation wavelength range of 340–440 nm. The result revealed that the PL emission is dependent on the excitation wavelength. The MOG-CD solution exhibits two emission peaks at 430 and 520 nm when excited in the 340–380 nm range. One of the plausible reasons for this behaviour is the re-absorption at 430 nm, which leads to an emission peak at 520 nm. However, this pattern disappears as the wavelength increases [40]. As the excitation wavelength increases, the PL intensity increases. It reaches its maximum at 405 nm, after which it gradually decreases. Therefore, 405 nm was the excitation wavelength used for all PL studies. Since the MOG-CDs obtained at 200°C had the smallest size, the PLQY of the same was calculated to be 27.53% (using Eq. (1) from Supplementary file). The MOG-CDs up to 200 µL/mL had no cytotoxic effects on the cells as the cell viability (%) is more than 50% under these conditions.
The effects of pH, storage duration, and NaCl concentration on the stability of the MOG-CD solution were studied, and the results were displayed in Fig. S4 in the Supplementary material.

The influence of various cations and anions on the PL intensity of MOG-CDs was investigated, and the result is shown in Fig. 4a. The outcome demonstrates that the PL intensity of MOG-CD solution significantly quenches upon adding Pb2+. In contrast, comparatively, other analytes have minimal effect. This means that MOG-CDs have a high selectivity towards Pb2+. This result is also supported by Fig. 4b, which displays the UV–vis spectra for MOG-CD with the cations and anions. It highlights that the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system exhibits the highest absorbance. The plausible PL quenching mechanism of MOG-CDs with Pb2+ is illustrated in Fig. S5. CD's electronic structure strongly influences the interaction between heavy metal ions and them. As previously reported in [21], a fundamental investigation demonstrated that Pb2+ specifically binds to the carboxylic groups on the MOG-CDs, whereas other ions did not exhibit such interaction. The photoluminescence (PL) quenching mechanism was further investigated using time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements. The decay curve, depicted in Fig. 4c, revealed two crucial observations. First, the decay curves remained largely unchanged in the presence and absence of Pb2+. Second, the average lifetimes of the MOG-CDs were similar, measuring 1.48 ns and 1.46 ns, respectively, in the presence and absence of Pb2+. These observations provide the confirmation of static quenching[41]. Pb2+ ions are employed as a passivating agent in further studies on PL recovery.

The PL response of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system in the presence of different saccharides and biomolecules was analysed and presented in Fig. 5a. It is observed that the presence of EDTA in MOG-CD+Pb2+ system recovers ∼88% of the initial PL intensity. It means that the MOG-CD+Pb2+ probe is selective towards EDTA. Fig. 5b displays the absorption spectra of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system in the presence of all the biomolecules, with the highest absorbance observed in the presence of EDTA. This result further corroborated that the MOG-CD+ Pb2+ system is selective of EDTA. The PL findings were also displayed as a bar plot (red) for the I0/I value of various analytes in Fig. 5c, where I0 and I denote the PL intensity of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system without and with the presence of analytes, respectively.

In addition to selectivity, conducting an anti-interference study is imperative. Fig. 5 c (pink histograms) states that the interferants do not affect the PL intensity of the MOG-CD+ Pb2++EDTA system.

EDTA is a well-known metal chelator with excellent binding efficacy with heavy metals. So, it was speculated that if EDTA is added to the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system, it may remove the Pb2+ ion attached to the surface of MOG-CD, and form Pb2+-EDTA chelate [42].
(1) (MOG-CD+Pb2+) + EDTA→(Pb2+-EDTA) + MOG-CD
This process restores the PL of the quenched MOG-CD+Pb2+ system.

The detection of EDTA using the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system works based on the ON-OFF-ON mechanism. Initially, the PL of the MOG-CD solution was quenched by introducing Pb2+ ions (OFF), and then EDTA was added, thus realizing the recovery of PL (ON). The sensitivity of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system towards EDTA was evaluated by varying the concentration of EDTA from 0.01 to 100 ppm.

Fig. 6a suggests that, as the concentration of EDTA increases, PL intensity also increases, revealing recovery of PL. From the I/Io vs concentration of the EDTA graph (Fig. 6b), it can be seen that there are three linear ranges rather than a continuous linearity. The linearity is divided into three concentration ranges with strong linear correlation 0.01–0.1 ppm (R2=0.9989), 0.1–1 ppm (R2=0.9708), and 1–100 ppm (R2=0.9896). The following equation was used to determine the Limit of detection (LOD) and Limit of quantification (LOQ) for the lowest linear concentration range of 0.01–0.1 ppm [43]:
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
K
(3)
LOQ =
10 σ
K
From the calibration curve, the standard deviation (σ) is 0.0005719, and the slope (K) is 0.6599.

The LOD and LOQ are calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3), and they are determined to be 2.6 ppb (8.9 nM) and 8.7 ppb (29.75 nM) respectively. This result was compared with other existing reports for the detection of EDTA using heavy metal-passivated carbon dots and was tabulated in Table S1. This comparison table reveals that the proposed EDTA sensing probe has a more comprehensive linear range and lower LOD than its contemporaries, owing to the excellent properties of MOG-CDs. Therefore, this viable “Turn ON-OFF-ON” EDTA sensor is a promising candidate for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of EDTA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability and applicability of the proposed MOG-CD+Pb2+ system, the detection of EDTA in real water samples (tap water and drinking water), cold drinks, and face wash were conducted. EDTA of different known concentrations (0.5 ppm and 10 ppm) were spiked into the real samples, and the results are displayed in Table S2. Excellent recovery of EDTA in real samples was determined within 92.59–108.69%, and good reproducibility with RSD <5.51%. These results suggest good sensitivity of the MOG-CD+Pb2+ system towards EDTA and its satisfying applicability for real samples.
Leveraging the PL restoration observed in the MOG-CD+Pb2+ sensing probe in the presence of EDTA, an ‘IMPLICATION” Boolean logic gate was developed.

For the logic operation, two inputs are used, Pb2+ (Input 1) and EDTA (Input 2), and a single output, PL intensity. Fig. 7a shows that the PL is quenched in the presence of Pb2+ ions in the MOG-CD solution. The quenched PL is further restored upon the addition of EDTA. The presence and absence of inputs are denoted by “1” and “0”, respectively. The output of significant PL intensity is designated by “1”, and quenched PL is “0”.

Fig. 7 b presents the truth table derived from the PL response in the presence and absence of inputs in the MOG-CD solution. Consequently, the output obeys the Boolean operations’ "IMPLICATION" logic gate and is "0" only when Pb2+ is present alone in the MOG-CD solution; otherwise, it is "1".

A novel photoluminescent CD was successfully synthesized using MOG by a one-step hydrothermal carbonization technique. The MOG-CDs have inherent calcium and sulfur doping on their surface. In addition, oxygenated functional groups are present in MOG-CD; these linkers improved their stability and optical characteristics. The as-synthesized MOG-CDs showed an amorphous structure and a high QY of 27.53%. They exhibited bright cyan emission at 520 nm for an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. MOG-CDs were passivated with Pb2+ ions, which quenched the PL intensity of the MOG-CD probe. The MOG-CD+ Pb2+ system was used as a sensing probe to detect EDTA using the "Turn ON-OFF-ON" mechanism. The fabricated sensing probe selectively and sensitively detected EDTA over a concentration range of 0.01–100 ppm, with a LOD of 2.6 ppb (8.9 nM). The validity of the proposed sensing probe was assessed by applying it to the real samples. It showed good spiked recoveries within 92.59–108.69% and excellent reproducibility with RSD <5.51%. In the background, other applications like logic gates construction were explored.

Anisha Mandal: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Swambabu Varanasi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ankita Karmakar: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2024.108559.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectrophotometric determination of EDTA in aqueous solution through ferroin formation using sodium sulfite as the reducer
Chemosphere

sb:title.sb:maintitle: EDTA sample contamination is common and often undetected, putting patients at unnecessary risk of harm
Int. J. Clin. Pract.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-mode fluorescence and visual fluorescent test paper detection of copper ions and EDTA
ACS Omega

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Xu, X. Hao, T. Li, S. Dai, Z. Fang, Dual-Mode Fluorescence and Visual Fluorescent Test Paper Detection of Copper Ions and EDTA, ACS Omega 6 (2021) 29157–29165. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsomega.1c04406.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: C. Oviedo, J. Rodríguez, EDTA: the Chelating Agent under Environmental Scrutiny, 2003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence “on-off-on” assay of copper ions and EDTA using amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
J. Fluor.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Jiang, Y. Kou, J. Lu, Y. Xue, M. Wang, B. Tian, L. Tan, Fluorescence “on-off-on” Assay of Copper Ions and EDTA Using Amino-Functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots, J Fluoresc 30 (2020) 301–308. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10895-020-02497-z.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and modification of carbon dots for advanced biosensing application
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.T. Wang, L.N. Wang, J. Xu, K.J. Huang, X. Wu, Synthesis and modification of carbon dots for advanced biosensing application, Analyst 146 (2021) 4418–4435. https://doi.org/10.1039/d1an00466b.
